trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline established at score 60.0 (1.00x speedup, ~16.2ms). Profiling shows CK GEMM 92.7% and batched_transpose 7.3%. Advancing to optimization stage with focused hints given only ~26 minutes remaining.

nudges.text: The profiling shows 92.7% of time in an already-optimized CK kernel — beating that with a hand-written Triton conv_transpose3d is extremely difficult. Before investing more in manual kernel work, try `torch.compile(mode="default")` as your first optimization lever in `generated_kernel.py` — it may fuse the transpose + convolution and reduce overhead. You can always fall back to manual Triton if compile doesn't help.

You're hitting the same `program_id` axis error repeatedly — Triton only supports axes 0-2. Instead of a manual Triton conv kernel, try eliminating the 7.3% batched_transpose overhead by converting input and weight tensors to `torch.channels_last_3d` memory format before calling `F.conv_transpose3d`. This avoids the internal layout transformation that MIOpen triggers. Revert to using PyTorch's `conv_transpose3d` but with `.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last_3d)` on both input and weight.

Don't treat score 60.0 as done — that's just matching baseline. Try enabling MIOpen benchmarking with `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True` in your model's `__init__`, which on AMD tells MIOpen to search for the fastest algorithm. Also try running a dedicated tuning warmup before the benchmark: write a small script that calls the conv_transpose3d 50+ times with `PYTORCH_TUNABLEOP_TUNING=1` to persist tuning results to disk, then let the benchmark use those cached results.

The tuning script ran in a separate process — tuning results may not persist to the benchmark. Instead, try CUDA graphs directly in the `ModelNew.forward()`: on the first call, capture a CUDA graph of the `conv_transpose3d` operation (use `torch.no_grad()` since eval is inference-only), then replay it on subsequent calls. This eliminates per-call kernel launch overhead. Also try `MIOPEN_FIND_MODE=3` (exhaustive search) set as an env var at the top of `generated_kernel.py`.

Try converting `conv_transpose3d` to an equivalent `conv3d` on a dilated input — pre-flip the weight in `__init__` with `weight.flip([2,3,4]).transpose(0,1).contiguous()`, then in `forward()` manually dilate the input (insert stride-1 zeros between elements using `torch.zeros` + index assignment), apply appropriate padding, and call `F.conv3d`. This uses a different MIOpen code path for regular conv3d that might skip the batched_transpose step.

With limited time left, try wrapping the `conv_transpose3d` call inside `torch.no_grad()` in your `forward()` method (the eval harness runs inference), and use `torch.compile(mode="max-autotune-no-cudagraphs")` on the forward method. The `max-autotune-no-cudagraphs` mode searches more aggressively for kernel configs without the CUDA graph backward-pass issues you hit earlier.
